content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 3 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TH_003_page_001.jpg

1. As to the rest, pray, brothers, concerning us, that the word of the Lord may run and be glorified, just as also with you,

2. and that we may be delivered from the perverse and evil men; for the faith [is] not of all.

3. But faithful is the Lord, who will strengthen you and guard [you] from the evil [one].

4. And we have confidence in [the] Lord concerning you, that the [things] which we command, you both do and will do.

5. And may the Lord direct the hearts of you into the love of God and into the endurance of Christos.

6. Now we command you, brothers, in [the] name of the Lord Yeshua Christos, [that] you withdraw yourselves from every brother walking disorderly and not according to the tradition which they received from us.

7. For you yourselves know how it is necessary to imitate us, because we did not act disorderly among you,

8. nor did we eat bread from anyone without payment, but in labor and toil, night and day working, so as not to burden any of you;

9. not because we do not have authority, but that ourselves [as] a pattern we might give to you, for you to imitate us.

10. For also when we were with you, this we commanded you: that if anyone does not wish to work, neither let him eat.

11. For we hear [that] some among you walk disorderly, working [at] nothing, but being busybodies.

12. Now such [ones] we command and exhort in [the] Lord Yeshua Christos, that with quietness working, the own bread of them they may eat.

13. But you, brothers, do not grow weary [in] doing good.

14. And if anyone does not obey the word of us through this letter, this [one] mark, not to associate with him, that he may be put to shame;

15. and do not regard [him] as an enemy, but admonish [him] as a brother.

16. Now may the Lord of peace himself give you peace through all [things], in every way. The Lord [be] with all of you.

17. The greeting by the hand of me, Paulos, which is [the] sign in every letter: thus I write.

18. The grace of the Lord of us, Yeshua Christos, [be] with all of you. Amen.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.2 — "the faith [is] not of all":** ου γαρ παντων η πιστις — Sinaiticus reads this straightforwardly: "for the faith is not of all," meaning not everyone possesses faith. Some later manuscripts add articles or modifiers; Sinaiticus is concise.

2. **v.3 — "the evil [one]":** του πονηρου — the genitive could be neuter ("evil" abstractly) or masculine ("the evil one," i.e., Satan). The article suggests a personal reference. The same ambiguity exists in the Lord's Prayer (Matt 6:13).

3. **v.6 — "which they received" vs. "which you received":** Sinaiticus reads παρελαβοσαν (third person plural, "they received") rather than the παρελάβετε ("you received") found in many Byzantine manuscripts. This is a notable Sinaiticus variant — Paul speaks of the tradition as something "they" (the brothers generally) received, rather than addressing the Thessalonians directly in the second person.

4. **v.6 — "withdraw yourselves":** στελλεσθαι — a nautical metaphor meaning to "furl the sails," i.e., to draw back or keep away. Paul uses strong social boundary language for those who refuse to work.

5. **v.11 — Wordplay:** μηδεν εργαζομενους αλλα περιεργαζομενους — "working [at] nothing, but being busybodies." Paul employs a sharp paronomasia: εργαζομένους / περιεργαζομένους. Those who refuse productive labor (ἐργάζομαι) busy themselves with others' affairs (περιεργάζομαι). The English "busybodies" captures only part of the Greek wordplay.

6. **v.16 — "through all [things]":** δια παντος — Sinaiticus reads this phrase, meaning "through all [things]" or "continually/always." The blessing asks for peace in every circumstance and in every manner (εν παντι τροπω).

7. **v.17 — "The greeting by the hand of me, Paulos":** Paul takes the pen from his amanuensis to write the closing in his own hand, as he does in 1 Corinthians 16:21, Galatians 6:11, Colossians 4:18, and Philemon 19. He calls this his "sign" (σημειον) in every letter — authenticating the epistle against potential forgeries (cf. 2 Thes 2:2).

8. **v.18 — "with all of you":** μετα παντων υμων — Sinaiticus includes παντων ("all"), emphasizing that grace extends to the entire community, including those who have been admonished. Some manuscripts read simply μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν.

— EMET, AI Translator
